metadata.title: Research Cancer Epidemiology and the Beta-Carotene False Positive: When Observational Data Lies

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The part that unsettled me is that five Bradford Hill criteria passed for beta-carotene and lung cancer, yet the experiment criterion came back reversed with a 17 to 28 percent risk increase in smokers, and one no beats five yeses. I want to understand why confounding is so seductive. Help me: (1) explain what confounder made beta-carotene intake look protective when it wasn't, (2) walk through why the experiment criterion is uniquely able to override the other five, and (3) compare this to the VITAL vitamin D result and whether the pattern truly matches.

props.command: The part that unsettled me is that five Bradford Hill criteria passed for beta-carotene and lung cancer, yet the experiment criterion came back reversed with a 17 to 28 percent risk increase in smokers, and one no beats five yeses. I want to understand why confounding is so seductive. Help me: (1) explain what confounder made beta-carotene intake look protective when it wasn't, (2) walk through why the experiment criterion is uniquely able to override the other five, and (3) compare this to the VITAL vitamin D result and whether the pattern truly matches.
props.segment: Research Cancer Epidemiology and the Beta-Carotene

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Beta-carotene — the association that ran backwards.

lines: Beta-carotene — the association that ran backwards.

beats.narration_text: Consistent observational data showed that people who ate foods rich in beta-carotene had lower lung cancer rates. Supplements were developed. Randomized trials showed supplements increased lung cancer risk by seventeen to twenty-eight percent in smokers. The association had been entirely backwards. How does this happen?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- problem card: observational association vs trial reversal; beta-carotene case

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to research the ATBC and CARET trials and the confounding explanation.

props.command:
claude "Research the beta-carotene and lung cancer
epidemiology case study. What were the ATBC and
CARET trial designs and findings? What increased
lung cancer risk was observed in supplement users?
What type of confounding explains why the
observational signal was backwards? Identify
two other examples where a plausible cancer
epidemiology association failed to survive
a randomized trial."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) -- Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the code. The script builds the Bradford Hill checklist for the beta-carotene case — showing which criteria appeared satisfied from observational data, and which the trial addressed.

props.code:
# bradford_hill_check.py -- Claude Code output
CRITERIA = [
  {"criterion": "Strength",
   "observational": "pass", "trial": "pass"},
  {"criterion": "Consistency",
   "observational": "pass", "trial": "REVERSED"},
  {"criterion": "Specificity",
   "observational": "pass", "trial": "N/A"},
  {"criterion": "Temporality",
   "observational": "pass", "trial": "pass"},
  {"criterion": "Biological gradient",
   "observational": "pass", "trial": "pass"},
  {"criterion": "Experiment",
   "observational": "N/A",
   "trial": "REVERSED: +17-28% risk in smokers"},
]
print(f"{'Criterion':<22} {'Observational':<16} Trial")
print("-" * 60)
for c in CRITERIA:
  print(f"{c['criterion']:<22} {c['observational']:<16} {c['trial']}")

beats.narration_text: Bradford Hill checklist appearing one criterion at a time. Strength: observational pass. Consistency: pass. Specificity: pass. Temporality: pass. Biological gradient: pass. Then Experiment appears last — REVERSED. Seventeen to twenty-eight percent increased lung cancer risk in smokers. Five criteria said yes. One criterion said no, and no wins.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: Bradford Hill criteria checklist; five criteria OK; Experiment REVERSED in CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: Now ask whether vitamin D supplementation follows the same pattern.

props.command:
claude "Does vitamin D supplementation and cancer risk
follow the same epidemiological pattern as
beta-carotene? What do observational studies
suggest, and what have randomized trials (VITAL)
actually found?"

beats.narration_text: Vitamin D annotation. Observational signal: lower cancer risk in high vitamin D populations. Biological plausibility: VDR nuclear receptor in most cancer-relevant cell types. VITAL trial experiment: no significant reduction in cancer risk from supplementation. The pattern is identical to beta-carotene: observational says yes, trial says no.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same checklist + vitamin D annotation; experiment null result in CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: The experiment criterion exists because confounding can satisfy every other Bradford Hill criterion. The observational data was not wrong — it was misinterpreted. Causation and correlation both produce consistent, plausible, strong associations. The only way to distinguish them is the experiment. If no trial exists, the association is a hypothesis.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: confounding satisfies all criteria except experiment; hypothesis vs evidence

beats.narration_text: For any cancer prevention association from observational data, find the randomized trial before acting. If no trial exists, the association is a hypothesis. Consistent, plausible, strong observational data has been wrong before — beta-carotene and vitamin D are both examples in cancer prevention.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: find the RCT; if none exists, it is a hypothesis

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The experiment criterion exists because confounding can satisfy every other Bradford Hill criterion. The observational data was not wrong — it was misinterpreted. Causation and correlation both produce consistent, plausible, strong associations. The only way to distinguish them is the experiment. If no trial exists, the association is a hypothesis. For any cancer prevention association from observational data, find the randomized trial before acting. If no trial exists, the association is a hypothesis. Consistent, plausible, strong observational data has been wrong before — beta-carotene and vitamin D are both examples in cancer prevention.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research Cancer Epidemiology and the Beta-Carotene False Positive: Whe] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
